topic: An artist discovers someone has tattooed their work
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt like interrogation lamps, exposing every smudge and hesitant line of the canvas. Elias stared at the finished piece, a swirling vortex of bruised purples and frantic, jagged lines. He ran a trembling hand over the surface, the texture slick beneath his fingertips. Then he saw it—a tiny, almost invisible detail near the lower edge, a perfectly rendered, miniature rose, inked in a shade of crimson that shouldn't exist in his palette. A cold knot tightened in his chest, pulling the air from his lungs. He leaned closer, his breath catching in a shallow gasp. It wasn't his handiwork. It was someone else’s signature, woven into the very fabric of his creation. A frantic urge to tear the painting down washed over him, quickly replaced by a paralyzing need to understand who dared to claim his vision.

[story 2]

Maya paced the cramped studio floor, the silence amplifying the frantic drumming in her ears. The charcoal sketch lay abandoned on the easel, a landscape of desolate, jagged peaks. Her knuckles were white where she gripped the edge of the wooden desk. She kept glancing toward the corner where her newest work sat, a vibrant portrait of a weeping figure. As she approached, her eyes snagged on the subject's collarbone. There, stark against the pale skin, was a delicate, almost surgical tattoo—a stylized raven in flight. A wave of nausea rolled over her. This wasn't an accident; this was a trespass. Her carefully constructed world of originality felt suddenly fragile, exposed. She dropped to her knees, her shoulders slumping as she stared, the vibrant colors of her own life seeming to drain away, leaving only a hollow ache in her stomach.

[story 3]

Arthur traced the outline of his latest oil painting with a fingertip, the pigment cool and unresponsive. He had poured months of sleepless nights into capturing the exact melancholy of twilight. When he finally stepped back, a sharp intake of breath escaped him. On the shadowed expanse of the figure's shoulder, a pattern of intricate, swirling script—a language he didn't recognize—was clearly visible. It was undeniably someone else’s mark, a deliberate insertion into his narrative. A tremor started in his hands, making the brush slip from his grasp. He stood rigid, his gaze fixed on the mark, the realization settling in his gut like a stone. He felt utterly undone, the effort of creation suddenly feeling like a performance he had inadvertently co-authored with a stranger.
